mental_health.description: There is an organisational chart or document that describes the lines of authority and accountability between the national programme governance structure and the districts’ health management teams.
Those responsible for governance (MOH&W level) approve and make public the programme’s mission statement.
Those responsible for governance set the goals for improving the mental health of all people living in the country through specific programme objectives.
Those responsible for governance (MOH&W level) ensure approval of strategic policies and strategic plans to operate a Mental Health Services programme.
Those responsible for governance (MOH&W level) approve the budget and allocate resources required to meet the programmes’ mission.
Those responsible for national programme governance receive and act upon reports (measurement and evaluation data) of the programme, at least quarterly.
Those responsible for national programme governance receive and act upon reports on the programmes’ risk management data at least quarterly.
The National Programme Coordinator for the programme has the education and experience to match the requirements in the position description.
The National Programme Coordinator coordinates the development and distribution of policies for programme management and implementation functions.
The National Programme Coordinator monitors compliance with applicable laws, regulations and programme-specific policies.
The National Programme Coordinator guides the District Health Management Teams in meeting the programme’s mission, goals, and objectives.
The National Programme Coordinator determines the programme related care, treatment, and services it provides at different levels of care.
The National Programme Coordinator implements processes to manage and control human, financial and other resources required to deliver programme-specific services.
The National Programme Coordinator ensures that the required physical facilities, installations and equipment are available to provide the specified programme services.
There is a documented procedure to guide the delegation of authorities within the programme.
The National Programme Coordinator ensures the implementation of risk management processes and activities.
The National Programme Coordinator ensures implementation of processes for programme-specific quality management and improvement.
The National Programme Coordinator implements processes to monitor the quality of programme specific clinical services.
The leaders of the programme at national and district level are formally identified.
The leaders of the programme identify the desired or expected programme related services for a specific catchment population within the health district.
The programme leaders work collaboratively to develop and implement the strategic plan and operational goals of the national Healthcare programme.
Progress in implementing the programme delivery plan is monitored at regular intervals according to national strategic goals and M&E framework.
The programme leaders meet at regular intervals to determine resources required to meet the needs of the catchment population.
The district’s programme leaders ensure that policies and procedure s guide and support the operational activities and management of the programme.
Policies and procedures guide the development, retention and destruction of all programme documentation including patient-related information, legal, managerial and research documentation.
There is a mechanism to ensure that policies are known and implemented.
A Mental Health Service programme appropriate diagnostic laboratory service is offered at all levels of health service delivery in the district.

A current manual/guidelines and steps on how to use various Mental Health Service programme specific tests, read different test results and assure quality of services are available, distributed and implemented at all healthcare facilities.
Appropriate laboratory turn-around times for individual facilities have been established and monitored.
The district programme leaders have access to regular laboratory quality reports.
A Mental Health Service programme appropriate diagnostic imaging service is accessible at all levels of health service delivery in the district.
Appropriate turn-around times for individual facilities have been established and are monitored.
The district programme leaders have access to regular diagnostic imaging service provider quality reports.
Designated CSS service providers are available for all levels of health service delivery in the district.
Appropriate turn-around times for packs to and from individual CSS service providers have been established and are monitored.
The district programme leaders have access to regular CSS service provider quality reports.
A coordinated, district wide, plan is in place for the allocation, control and use of vehicles required for the programme.
Quality management processes are designed and implemented.
A centralised district level plan for the allocation, distribution and control of drugs, medication and consumables required for the programme is in place.
A coordinated, district wide, system for urgent temporary replacement of diagnostic equipment required for the programme is in place.
The ability of the central supply stores to meet the drugs, medication, consumables and equipment needs of the programme is monitored on a regular (at least quarterly) basis.
There is a system for calculating and predicting district-wide programme-specific stock turnover and requirements.
All facilities have access to an electronic as well as a paper-based back up supply chain management system.
Quality management processes are designed and implemented.
There is a mechanism for programme leaders to participate in the development and management of programme related budgets.
Processes are in place to aggregate data required by Programme Coordinators.
Contracts and other arrangements are monitored as part of the programmes’ quality management and improvement programme.
Patients are screened and referred as per policies.
The programme leaders ensure that programme-specific clinical policies, procedures and forms are compiled and indexed and ensure their circulation, recall, archiving and review.
Programme-specific policies, procedures, protocols and/or forms are available to support its practices across the entire programme care continuum.

The programme leaders ensure implementation of amendments to clinical protocols, procedures, guidelines and forms in response to changes in evidence-based national or international guidelines, systematic review of existing evidence or results of analysed data and clinical audits.
District programme leaders ensure that a district wide, coordinated orientation plan for the programme’s healthcare professionals is implemented.
There is a district wide, post-basic training strategy for the programme which ensures that all personnel regularly update their knowledge and skills.
Data on training outcomes and competencies are monitored and analysed.
Opportunities for quality improvement of the training programme are identified and action plans implemented.
Compliance with programme-specific guidelines and protocols are audited.
Information systems for the programme are developed and implemented.
The programme leaders, in collaboration with subject matter experts, identify key indicators and other information required to monitor quality assurance and improvement processes.
Programme leaders identify key measures to monitor the quality of clinical processes.
Confidentiality of data and information is maintained.
Aggregated data and information is used to support management of the programme.
Aggregated data and information is used to identify opportunities for quality improvement.
Data or information is contributed to national programme databases as required by law or regulation, where applicable.
Clinical audits are performed to evaluate the quality of care provided, drive improvements in service provision and monitor performance over time.
Professional performance is monitored as part of clinical monitoring.
Comparisons are made over time within the programme, among districts, and with national M&E goals and targets.
The data collected for monitoring and evaluation purposes is used to inform the development of processes to ensure that improvements are sustained over time.
Risk management strategies for the programme are developed and implemented.
A risk register, including programme-specific reportable operational and clinical risk categories, is developed and implemented.
There is evidence of ongoing documented monitoring of risks.
An appropriately qualified and experienced individual (Focal Person) has clearly defined responsibilities and accountability for all aspects of the service.
The Focal Person compiles and provides reports to the District Programme Coordinator and/or programme leaders for feedback on a regular basis.
At least one qualified person is available at all times (medical practitioner or registered nurse) to participate and/or provide guidance in the management of mental health emergencies.
An interdisciplinary programme team has been established and meets at regular intervals.
Minimum critical competencies required for any/all professional nurses, medical practitioners and other professionals providing basic, regular Mental Health care are identified and assessed.

Staffing norms are determined for the allocation of appropriately trained healthcare professionals required to meet the Mental Health Service programme’s scope of care, treatment and level of services it provides at all times during official facility hours. official facility hours.
The employees rendering Mental Health services have education, experience, training and certification consistent with the programme’s scope of care, treatment and services.
Responsibilities and accountabilities of the employees rendering Mental Health services are defined.

Mental Health Service specific orientation and training needs for designated healthcare professionals who render emergency Mental Health services are identified and communicated to the District Programme Coordinator.
Mental Health Service specific orientation and training for designated healthcare professionals who render emergency mental health services is implemented.
Critical competencies of employees that render mental health services are assessed through observation on an ongoing basis. This assessment is documented.
Where volunteers are used, they have received the required orientation and training relevant to the functions that they perform within the facility.
Where volunteers are used, they work under the guidance of the Facility Manager or his/her delegate in the employ of the facility.
Clinical practice guidelines and protocols required to meet the Mental Health Service programme’s scope of care, treatment and level of services are available.
Clinical practice guidelines and protocols are based on international and national care guidelines, clinical evidence and mental health subject matter expert team’s input.
The implementation of guidelines and protocols is monitored as part of a structured clinical audit.
Results of facility audits are provided to and analysed by the facility’s programme team and provided to district programme le aders.
Regular M&M (Morbidity and Mortality) meetings to discuss adverse patient outcomes and complications take place.
Policies, procedures and forms required to meet the mental health service programme’s scope of care, treatment and level of services are available and implemented.
In-service training is provided to personnel to ensure that they understand the intent and content of the policies and procedures.
The required furniture for mental health services is available and functional.
Medical equipment, instruments and supplies relevant to mental health service guidelines are available according to the most current protocol.
A preventive maintenance programme for all equipment and instruments is in place.
Deficiencies and needs are regularly documented and communicated by the Focal Person to facility and programme leaders.
Turnaround times for ordering and repairs are monitored and analysed.
Staff are trained and competent in the use of specialised medical equipment.
A functional kitchen with equipment appropriate for the level of the facility and programme services rendered is available.
Linen and laundry supplies and facilities are adequate for the level of service and volume of patients.
Policies and procedures ensure that personnel receive guidance in the functions performed.
A system is in place to ensure that departmental policies and procedures are known and implemented
Patients and families have received orientation on sanitary requirements for linen and clothing and infection prevention and control procedures.
There is an adequate number of suitably trained housekeeping personnel to provide a safe and effective service.
Housekeeping policies and procedures relevant to cleaning of various service areas and equipment are available and implemented.
Spill kits for cleaning hazardous material and blood and body fluid spills are available.
A designated, registered professional has clearly defined responsibilities and accountability for all aspects of the pharmaceutical service

Cooperation exists between the programme Focal Person, programme leaders and the pharmacy and/or other relevant staff in the facility to ensure safe prescribing, ordering, storage and dispensing of programme-specific medication.
There is a collaborative effort to develop and monitor additional medication policies and procedures specific to the mental health service.

Functional pharmaceutical refrigerators and/or cool bags designated for storage of vaccines and other temperature-sensitive medication are available and monitored to ensure maintenance of the cold chain.
Medication identified for special control by legislation or policy is securely stored and accurately accounted for in accordance with Botswana laws, regulations or policy.

Appropriate drugs and medication are stocked and readily available for ordering and prescribing according to the Botswana drug formulary and protocols and the facility’s level of care, mission, patient needs and services provided.
There is a process to ensure the safe and legal prescribing/ordering of medication.
The facility’s professional staff adhere to laws, regulations and approved practice guidelines when dispensing and administering medication.
Medication is stored, dispensed and administered in a manner that ensures safety and effectiveness.
Medication orders must be recorded in the patient's file and signed by the ordering person with his/her name, signature, designation, date and time.
The facility provides point-of-care laboratory testing essential to the immediate classification and treatment of the patient.
Protocols for requesting investigations required for the management of specific mental health conditions and complications must be available.
Turnaround times for laboratory results are monitored.
A laboratory specimen register is kept updated and missing results are followed up.
Trained Health Education Assistants (HEAs) are available to provide training to patients and the general population in the facility’s catchment population.
The facility’s mental health service team promotes, supports and participates in mental health service related community outreach projects.
Printed health education material, in a language common to the catchment population, is distributed in key areas in the community.
Language and culture appropriate patient educational material for the mental health service programme are available.
Patients, families and caregivers receive information about the staff responsible for the delivery of their care, treatment, and services.
Individualised patient education is provided at each interaction and documented in the patient record.
The medical record captures comprehensive information about the mental health care patient.
Where there is an EHR system in place, data correlates with the patient–carried medical record
Measures to ensure security and confidentiality of information are implemented according to Botswana laws and regulations.
All patients are triaged and categorised upon first contact at the facility and the category is documented
All staff are trained on emergency signs and priority signs, and triage posters are available for easy reference.
Step-by-step protocols for assessment of each of the emergency and priority signs are developed, implemented are used for competency assessments.
Step-by-step protocols for treatment of symptoms are developed, implemented and used for competency assessments.
All assessments, treatment and follow-up care are documented in the medical record in detail.
Medication for emergency and priority treatment are provided as per approved protocol
Criteria and processes for referral to a higher level of care must be in place.
Organisational policies and procedures for assessing patients on arrival at the facility and during ongoing care are implemented.
Only those individuals permitted by applicable laws and regulations or by registration perform the assessments.
Assessments are performed within appropriate time frames and comprehensively documented in the patient's records according to organisational policy.
Each patient has a documented initial assessment which meets organisation policy.
The initial assessment result in a diagnosis and the formulation of an individualised plan of care for the patient.
Patient assessment data and information are analysed and integrated by those responsible for the patient’s care.
The patient and/or his or her family participate in the decisions regarding the priority needs to be met.
The patient's response to care and therapeutic interventions is documented in the patient record.
Any patient care meetings or other discussions are noted in the patient’s record.
All procedures and diagnostic tests and reason why they were ordered are performed, and written into the patient's record.
Only those permitted to write orders do so.
There are documented reassessments of patients according to organisational policy at intervals appropriate to their condition, plan of care and individual needs.
A detailed consent policy in accordance with Botswana acts and regulations guides consent for outpatient treatment and other procedures.
Patients and their families or decision-makers receive adequate, clear, understandable information to enable them to participate in care decisions.
Patients know the identity of the physician or other practitioners responsible for their care.
When treatments or procedures are planned, patients know who is authorised to perform the procedure or treatment
Patients and families participate in care decisions to the extent they choose.
The information provided is recorded, with the record of the patient having provided written or verbal consent.
There is documented participation of the patient with his or her family or significant other(s) in group therapy, as appropriate.
Policies and procedures for identified high risk patients and procedures which include at least a) - e) in the standard intent above, are available.
Staff are trained and implement the policies and procedures to guide care.
Patients receive care consistent with the policies and procedures.
Routine infection prevention and control (IPC) protocols are available and implemented.
Education for prevention of secondary infections and infection control procedures for home is provided.
A mental health service event reporting process and register is available and up to date.
Information management processes meet the programme’s internal and external information needs.
Aggregated data and information is used to monitor and support the mental health service programme’s quality improvement activities.
The facility’s improvement opportunities are identified and prioritised based on performance data comparisons against district and national target ranges.

The programme plans process and performance improvement activities to encompass multiple disciplines and/or settings from triage, assessment, classification, treatment and referral and/or follow-up stages.
The facility’s mental health service team acts on improvement opportunities.

The facility’s Focal Person and programme leaders meet at regular intervals, at least twice a year, to evaluate clinical care practices and protocols and to identify opportunities for performance improvement and progress on implemented improvement projects.
